## [03:01 - 06:04]

But just a few use cases for avatars to get your wheels turning. So I think an important distinction to make with avatars, there's really two sides to a customer avatar. So the first is demographics and demographics are what we have normally used to describe other groups of humans, right? So demographics are things like she's a woman over the age of 50 or he's a man, you know, over the age of 60, right? Or they're older, they're retired. These are all demographic ways of describing people based off their gender, their age, you know, maybe even their ethnicity. And although this is very important, I want you to understand that customer avatars and ad targeting is much more than demographics. So in the past, right, 40 or 50 years ago when you could just buy print ads or you could just purchase billboards, right? We didn't have the internet. We've relied on demographic marketing, right? We wanted to target women. We got in the magazine that women read, right? Things were much more simple then. Nowadays, with the data that we are all giving the internet and platforms like Facebook or Google, there's so much more that we now know as marketers about our avatars. And there's, you know, there's a much deeper way to reach these people. It's important to understand the demographics or at least have a loose sense for when you're writing ad copy or if you're imagining yourself sitting there speaking to this person when you're coming up with your marketing campaigns. But what's really important, the other side of these avatars is the intent and interest. So I care much more about the intent and the interests of your market than what they look like.
